# Questions in Livestream 211

Live Stream #211: When Your Guest Is A Gas Bag...

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:40</a> | Given the clan interpretation, how should one interpret Noah's 120 years to build the ark in Genesis 6:3? | The 120 years is treated as a countdown to the flood rather than the building period. Under a clan reading, Noah's household or posterity could also be involved in the ark work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=823" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:43</a> | Do other ancient Near Eastern nations put themselves in the harsh light Israel does when it turns its back on Yahweh? | Not normally. Other nations might blame a defeated king or accuse enemies of abandoning gods, but Falk says whole-nation self-rebuke is not typical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:03</a> | Did other ancient Near Eastern nations have a national deity as involved as Yahweh, directly interacting in history? | Sometimes victories were credited to gods intervening, but defeats were not usually blamed on the nation's own disobedience to its deity; Falk treats that as distinctive to Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1089" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:09</a> | Do other ancient Eastern faiths depict their deities as directly giving laws or worship to their nations? | Not typically. Egypt worked by a principle of order rather than a law code, while Mesopotamian law was descriptive precedent rather than prescriptive legislation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1281" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:21</a> | Is the abandonment of Avaris in the mid-19th dynasty good evidence for the Exodus, and does BTAC make that connection? | Falk says it is good corroborating evidence, but BTAC does not make that connection because he ties the Exodus to the mid-12th century through the four-room house evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1486" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:46</a> | Do you find super-late daters or early daters worse? | He finds early daters more frustrating because they generally work from less evidence and often begin from a fixed traditional position, though super-late daters also ignore key evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1700" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:20</a> | What are your views on Douglas Petrovich's 2013 argument for pinpointing the Egyptian abandonment of Avaris in the mid-18th dynasty? | Falk rejects it strongly. He says the palace abandonment does not show the whole city was abandoned, because Asiatic occupation continued in the temple district into the 19th dynasty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=1963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:43</a> | What are the urban population estimates for Avaris in the 18th to 19th dynasty? | Falk cites BTAC's estimate of roughly 5,000 to 50,000 people, based on pottery production, while noting the range is very broad. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:23</a> | Why is Amenhotep II the focus point for so many Exodus timelines? | Falk traces it to James Orr's early-date argument and later attempts to identify Amenhotep II as the Exodus pharaoh, but he says those arguments depend on unsupported claims about the oppression pharaoh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:02</a> | Would the wandering period not be exactly, or even roughly, 40 years? | Falk says it is not exact; the number functions as a vague or idiomatic estimate, so it could be near 40 without being precisely 40. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:13</a> | In Genesis 9:20-23, what did Ham do wrong? Was it voyeurism or a euphemism involving Noah's wife? | Falk argues it was not mere voyeurism. He reads "uncovering nakedness" as a euphemism involving Noah's wife, while cautioning that the woman was not necessarily Ham's mother. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=2925" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:45</a> | What is the meaning of Psalm 14, especially "the fool has said in his heart there is no God"? | Falk says the phrase can mean either denying God's existence or acting as if God is not present to judge. The Psalm begins with the fool but broadens to humanity's need for redemption. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=3233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:53</a> | What are your thoughts on icons and icon veneration? | He calls icon veneration an accretion: abiblical but not necessarily unbiblical. He doubts its efficacy while distinguishing that from saying it is against Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=3462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:42</a> | In Romans 11:26, who is meant by Israel? | Falk reads Israel as the Israelites, contrasted with Gentiles. Once the fullness of the Gentiles comes in, the partial hardening is lifted and the Israelite remnant comes to salvation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4215" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:15</a> | Does the Quran's claim that Egyptians practiced crucifixion as capital punishment fit the images of people on vertical and horizontal posts at Kom Ombo? | Falk says crucifixion in Egypt is a Hellenistic import, not a native Egyptian practice. He says Kom Ombo is a Ptolemaic temple and earlier Egyptian capital punishments were different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4456" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:16</a> | Were the Hebrews only in Avaris, or did they also settle in the larger area of Goshen? | Falk says the Semitic Asiatics who became Israel were spread through the Goshen area, including Avaris and surrounding pasture regions, with most in the Nile Delta. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:32</a> | Were Israelites at Elephantine Island during the Exodus period? | Falk says no; he places Jewish presence at Elephantine later, after Nebuchadnezzar's conquest and the assassination of Gedaliah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=4745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:05</a> | If Matthew 10:28 says the soul is destructible while Matthew 25 and Revelation speak of eternal fire, what is the second death and do we cease to be? | Falk rejects annihilationism here. He reads "destroy" as bring to ruin, and the second death as final separation from God rather than ceasing to exist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=5625" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:45</a> | Is there no postmortem or postjudgment salvation? | Falk says not for those who had a fair choice and knowingly rejected God. He leaves room for grace where there was no real choice, but says judgment closes the ordinary option. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=5705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:05</a> | Why would people change their minds after rejecting God while willingly knowing the truth? | Falk says they would not; if someone knows the truth and chooses to reject it anyway, there is no reason to expect a later change of mind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=5740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:40</a> | Does eternal life mean more than eternal existence? | Yes. Falk distinguishes eternal life as Zoe, fullness and quality of life in relationship, from mere bios or existence; eternal death is eternal relational brokenness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=6142" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:22</a> | How did you calculate the 2.5% tax figure for ancient Israel? | Falk says it comes from the Talmud and Jewish historical tradition about tax burdens from the monarchic period into the Herodian period, while noting the Talmud is not always correct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=6205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:25</a> | How can Hebrew be so versatile that one translator renders a word as drift and another as bentness or brokenness? | He explains this through lexical range. Hebrew has a small vocabulary, so words often carry broader semantic ranges than languages with more lexemes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=6837" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:57</a> | How long have you been on the super-soldier serum, given your work ethic and past jobs? | He answers humorously, then says he gets a lot done largely because he does not own or watch television and uses that time for work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7222" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:22</a> | Is the Shekinah God's spirit or word? | Falk equates the Shekinah glory with the Holy Spirit: the presence or essence of God within holy space such as the temple or tabernacle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7279" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:19</a> | How are Moody's language programs? | He says the language programs are okay, but strongly warns against Moody because he views the school as legalistic and restrictive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7546" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:46</a> | Is Moody's restrictive policy structure even legal? | Falk says yes because attendance is voluntary. He calls the policies shortsighted and unhealthy, but says they can be legal in that setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7822" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:22</a> | Did Ramesses II actually get a passport? | Yes. Falk explains that a passport or transport document was issued so the mummy could be transported to Paris for conservation under European rules for human remains. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=7923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:03</a> | In Deuteronomy 21:3-4, why that kind of heifer, and is the neck broken because blood was already spilled? | Falk says the ritual addresses bloodguilt on the land when a murderer is unknown. The unused heifer is ritually pure and serves as a costly absolution ceremony for communal innocence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=8270" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:50</a> | How does Deuteronomy 21:15-16 relate to Jacob's treatment of Joseph and Reuben? | Falk says the law closes a loophole Jacob used. Jacob effectively gave Joseph the firstborn double portion through Ephraim and Manasseh, even though Reuben was the biological firstborn. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=8653" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:13</a> | In Deuteronomy 21:18, what kind of rebelliousness is in view? | Falk says this is not a disobedient child but an adult son engaged in serious ritual and social rebellion, such as idolatry, violence, theft, drunkenness, and gluttony that harms others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=8939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:59</a> | Does 1 Corinthians 1:13 mean they baptized in the name of Jesus? | Yes, but Falk says Paul's point is that baptism by Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or others should not create factions because all are baptized in Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=9233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:53</a> | Why is partialism a heresy if infinite parts of God would still be infinite? | Falk says the issue is fullness of essence. Each person of the Trinity must have the fullness of the divine essence, not merely an infinite part of it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=9427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:07</a> | Why was Eve given her name after the fall in Genesis 3:20 rather than when she was first created? | Falk says the text does not explain fully, but Eve's name is tied to being mother of the living, which fits after expulsion and the beginning of offspring; she may also have had another earlier name. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=9955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:55</a> | Do modern Hebrew learners need to worry about second tenses? | No. Falk says modern Hebrew does not have second tenses, and even in classical Hebrew they are rare and not fully understood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:16</a> | Have you cosplayed, and if so, as what character? | He says he cosplayed once in college as Larva from Vampire Princess Miyu, and also did Renaissance fairs, though he does not count those as cosplay. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10273" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:13</a> | What are your favorite hard and soft cheeses? | He names Chaource, Cambozola, and Manchego among his favorite hard cheeses and says he likes ricotta among soft cheeses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10496" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:56</a> | Have you ever had shawarma? | Yes, once. He found it unremarkable and prefers gyros, especially because he likes the spiced lamb and tzatziki. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:10</a> | Why is the genealogy so different between Matthew 1 and Luke 3:23-4? | Falk says they serve different purposes: one gives the legal/adoptive claim through the father and the other the genetic claim through the mother, intersecting at points. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:42</a> | How do we know the killing of males would last throughout the pharaoh's reign? | He clarifies that it would not necessarily last through a long reign, but in the relevant case the king's short reign would limit how long the decree operated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=10879" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:19</a> | What do you think of the grammatical-historical-literal hermeneutic? | Falk likes the grammatical-historical method and calls it reliable, but says it is not necessarily literalist because genre, history, grammar, and context shape interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:12</a> | How semantically hazy are Greek participles in the genitive case? | He says middle-voice Greek participles in the genitive are among the hardest cases and require context; without context, the word alone is not enough. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:02</a> | What are your thoughts on Luke's historical account when commentaries claim discrepancies? | Falk recommends using Kurt Aland's Synopsis of the Four Gospels to compare the Gospel accounts side by side before judging historical issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:09</a> | Can the Greek word for brother also mean cousin, especially in claims about Mary having children? | Falk says the cousin reading looks like a theological stretch. Greek has a specific word for cousin, and he says the brother language in context is better read as brothers and sisters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=11960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:20</a> | Are the Samaritans correct that Mount Gerizim, rather than Zion, was the place chosen by God for worship? | Falk says no. He sees Samaritan worship at Gerizim as later and the Samaritan Pentateuch's Gerizim emphasis as a later amendment, while still valuing the Samaritan Pentateuch as an important source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=12454" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:34</a> | What is your reaction to cannabis and cow-dung residue at a temple at Tel Arad, and could it be strange fire? | Falk says cow dung could fit strange fire as an unclean fire source, but he is skeptical about cannabis claims and warns that residue studies can be contaminated or overinterpreted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=12991" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:31</a> | Could you talk about historical errors in the Catholic Apocrypha? | He says the question is too broad to answer well without preparation. Judith is the first example that comes to mind, but he asks for a specific book and advance notice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=13195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:55</a> | Have you heard the argument that Jesus is Yahweh but God the Father is Most High and higher than Yahweh? | Falk identifies this as subordinationism and warns that rank language must be carefully defined so it does not become ontological subordination, subjugationism, Arianism, or ditheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=13654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:34</a> | Is the face of the Sphinx a Sub-Saharan African? | Falk says no. He says the missing nose makes that impression more plausible, but the restored features and dynasty context point to the Memphis area, not Sub-Saharan Africa. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=13746" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:06</a> | Does Romans 1:18-20 mean everyone propositionally knows God exists, or is the passage more nuanced? | Falk says it is more nuanced. Knowledge of God is available through creation, but sinful humanity corporately suppresses the truth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=14090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:50</a> | How many languages are left that still need Bible translation? | Falk says it is getting low, but many remaining languages are critically endangered with very few speakers, so some may die out before they can be fully recorded and translated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/-moIej_LjtE?t=14367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:27</a> | After the controversies and slander, have you struggled with academia, and how can viewers help? | Falk says the controversy damaged his academic standing despite later vindication and pushed him toward full-time YouTube; he says supporting his publishing work is the best help. |
